chunk_summary: This section focuses on therapeutic goals and indications for supplementation in managing moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in children under 5 years, emphasizing home diet therapy and community-based interventions to prevent wasting and improve nutritional outcomes.
learning_objectives: Understand the therapeutic goals for managing moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in children under 5 years.
Identify indications for supplementary feeding in cases of uncomplicated moderate wasting.
Explore community-based management strategies for addressing MAM, including home diet therapy.
Recognize the role of national and global guidelines in improving child nutrition outcomes.
key_facts: Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) affects children under 5 years and increases the risk of mortality and morbidity.
Community-based management of acute malnutrition (CMAM) is a key strategy recommended by NITI Aayog and UNICEF (2023).
Supplementary feeding is indicated for children with moderate wasting to prevent progression to severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
The National Health Mission emphasizes enhancing optimal infant and young child feeding (IYCF) practices to combat malnutrition.
Wasting and stunting are significant risk factors for child mortality, as highlighted in a pooled analysis published in PLOS ONE.
reference_quote: "Wasting and stunting are significant risk factors for mortality in children under 5 years." (PLOS ONE, https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0064636)
